teachers  leave  envelopes  in  the  homes  into  which  Relief  Society  members 
may  place  their  dimes  until  they  have  saved  the  $5;  others  are  leaving  small 
containers  of  different  types  and  styles  into  which  the  money  may  be  put. 
In  some  localities  it  is  recommended  that  jars  be  kept  in  conspicuous  places 
and  family  members  be  asked  to  place  pennies  or  nickels  in  as  they  can. 
This  is  a  good  plan  as  it  allows  the  entire  family  to  participate  and  recall 
the  faithful  work  of  a  grandmother  or  some  other  woman  member  of  the 
family  who  is  remembered  for  her  outstanding  Relief  Society  service. 
In  addition  to  collecting  individual  member  donations,  some  Relief 
Societies  are  taking  steps  to  raise  money  as  a  group  in  order  to  help  sisters 
who  may  find  it  difficult  to  make  a  $5  contribution.  One  ward  is  turning 
over  to  the  fund,  for  a  limited  time,  all  money  made  by  quilting;  another 
has  a  ''sale  table"  at  each  Relief  Society  meeting  where  handmade  articles 
are  on  sale  following  the  meeting.  Still  another  ward  has  a  food  table 
where  busy  housewives  can  purchase  homemade  delicacies  for  supper.  Some 
Relief  Societies  are  giving  concerts  or  parties  to  which  members  may  bring 
their  quotas.  These  are  just  a  few  of  the  many  ingenious  methods  being 
devised  by  Relief  Society  officers  in  raising  the  assigned  ward  quotas. 
The  most  successful  results  are  found  in  stakes  where  the  Priesthood 
is  actively  supporting  the  program.  In  many  wards  the  bishops  are  writing 
letters  endorsing  the  collection  which  are  left  in  the  homes  by  visiting 
teachers. 
Page  151', 'INTERPRETING THE RELIEF SOCIETY EMPLOYMENT PROGRAM
By Eva Williams Darger IN the minutes of the first meeting of the Female Relief Society of Nauvoo we find that those women assumed the responsibility of helping the needy to find suitable employment. As the organization has grown this work has continued, and thousands of women and girls have found pleasant and profitable employment through the Relief Society.
When it is recognized that paid workers in the home constitute the largest occupational group of women in our country, it is clear that we have a great responsibility to help place them.
Homemaking is a great art.
Household employees who are privileged to work in homes where there is a spirit of love, comradeship and fair play, learn attitudes toward homemaking which they themselves will carry on into their own lives as they marry and rear families.
Seventy per cent of the employees placed through the Relief Society are under thirty years of age. The majority are placed for domestic service.
There are many important things for young women who come to the city from smaller districts to consider: The health of the individual is of vital importance. It is advisable for a girl to have a complete physical examination before she leaves home.
References as to her character, background and qualifications from reliable members of her community should be obtained. She 'should be provided with funds sufficient to care for her needs until suitable employment is found. She should bring adequate clothing such as neat, modest dresses, shoes, stockings and underwear.
Some knowledge of the hotels, location of employment bureaus, addresses of friends or relatives should be had.
Some educational qualifications are necessary.
Systematic training in household tasks for all girls would be of great 72 benefit.
Where it is possible girls should complete their high school education. If household employment is chosen as the field of work, specialized training by which a girl may become a skilled worker, should be pursued. In this way efficient and well-trained workers, able to establish high standards of work would be available, instead of the unskilled laborers that are now for the most part all that are to be had. The director of the United States Employment service said at the National Conference of Social Work, "The biggest of all tasks today is restoring employment to workers, not merely finding the man or woman a job, but finding the right job for the man or woman." In order to accomplish this, vocational guidance should be given: to counsel and assist girls and women contemplating employment to obtain more insight into their own aptitudes, capacities, traits, skills, knowledge and background; to secure additional education or training for available employment opportunities; to make the most of educational, recreational and other opportunities provided by the community.
Someone standing between RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE these young women and society as a kind of two-way clearing station, bringing facts to the individual from society, and in turn bringing facts back to society that emerge out of the individual problems disclosed in the process is necessary.
With an understanding knowledge of human beings, skill in dealing with other people's problems, with the desire to strengthen and encourage the individual's faith in herself, this program of providing employment for women is being carried on. In working toward satisfactory employer-employee relations, much would be gained if the worker would do all in her power to maintain the highest standards of home life, to consider her relationships with the family confidential, refraining at all times from harmful gossip, to be careful of her personal appearance, respect her employer's home furnishings, guard her manners and language especially in the presence of children, guard her habits in order to maintain good health and to enjoy wholesome recreation, to remember the Golden Rule, "Do unto others as you would have them do unto " you. ADDRESS Bishop Joseph Wirthlin AM deeply interested in Relief Society. Perhaps I inherited this from my own mother. She was at one time, a Relief Society president, and as a young man I saw how a Relief Society functioned.
Many of its affairs came into our home. I saw much of the work that was done by the sisters of our ward in connection with this great work, and for that reason I have a warm spot in my heart for this great organization. I do not hesitate in saying to you that I do not think there is an organization anywhere in the world today that is comparable to it. It seems to me that your program includes everything that is good, lovely, fine, progressive, spiritual; and the organization has to its credit many outstanding accomplishments?accomplishments within the memory of all of us. I am sure if there ever was a time in the history of the Church when a Relief Society was needed, RELIEF SOCIETY CONFERENCE that time is today. We are facing serious problems that will require the united support of every organization of the Church, and I am confident that the Relief Society will respond to each call that comes, as it always has done, and the same fine success will attend its efforts. Relief Society offers many splendid opportunities to the women of our Church. We have many talented women who most graciously give their time and abilities to the organization for the benefit of those who are not so privileged. JI am sure that if it were not for the fine program that has been developed there would be many women in the remote parts of this Church who would have very few opportunities.
There comes to mind now two instances. One is of a woman who has lived in one of the Canadian stakes for thirty years, in the home which her husband constructed when they first went to that country?a log house with two rooms.
When she and her husband first went to Canada they had high ambitions.
They were young and vigorous people and battled with the soil on the bleak prairies of Canada. One year they were successful, then for three or four years drought came and destroyed their crops. The sum total of their experience is that theyare still in the same log house situated ten miles from the nearest town.
Their physical comforts are few, but a highlight in this woman's life has been the fact that she was given the opportunity of teaching the literary work in Relief Society. Her president gave her the opportunity of going to Provo, Utah, to further her studies. She counts that as the 753 greatest blessing that ever came into her life. If it had not been for the Relief Society she never would have had that opportunity, and the joy which she now has would never have come to her. The other case is of a lady who lives in one of our districts. I have known her for years. As a girl she was shy and timid. In the course of time she married and moved: to a small town in California where there was a branch of the Church. The president of the Relief Society appointed her to supervise the Social Service department of the organization. She had never had any experience along that line; her knowledge was limited, but she had ambitions; so she set to work, visited the public library and studied all available authorities on social service.
Today she is an outstanding worker in her community. It is quite possible that if this woman had not had the opportunity of rendering this service and overcoming her inferiority complex, she would not have developed to any great extent. The Relief Society organization of the Church afforded her this opportunity and she made the most of it. The opportunities being offered to the women of this Church are a great inspiration, and I am sure the sisters appreciate the privileges afforded them. THERE is something else Relief Society has done?something being done now? which is most outstanding. We talk about the depression and its effects upon people; we hear the First Presidency constantly telling us to be frugal. The Relief Society is heeding that advice and setting a wonderful example. One of the fundamentals 754 that has come to my attention is that of good home management. I happen to have had the privilege of working in the Salt Lake Region, where a study was made of conditions relative to home management. We found that in many instances where people were on relief the mother did not know how to sew, to bake bread, nor how to do many of the simple things.
Because of the lack of this knowledge the family found itself in poverty. So the Relief Society has taken up a project of teaching people how to make bread properly, how to do the washing correctly, how to do good sewing.
About a year ago in a well-to do Salt Lake City stake a survey was made to determine how many of the women were baking their own bread and doing their washing and sewing. As nearly as I can remember, about 65 per cent of the women were doing their own work, 67 percent their own washing, and 60 per cent their own sewing. The Relief Society has gone into the wards and taught people how to do these things. One Relief Society president told me that she experienced much pleasure in going down to her laundry room on Monday morning and doing her washing because the Relief Society program had dignified this duty.
Connection with teaching our people to follow the advice of the First Presidency of the Church I want to compliment the Relief Society for teaching our sisters the necessity of properly managing their homes. The Relief Society has some definite duties in connection with the Church Welfare Program. Personally I cannot conceive of  RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE any bishop trying to put over the Church Welfare Program without you?you are a part of it. If the program is to be successful you will have to be in it actively?you will have to be used. My experiences as a bishop and stake president have taught me, without any doubt, that the Relief Society has a very important part to play in the Church Welfare Program. It is true that we have the Priesthood quorums, and the Priesthood quorums of this Church should be so stimulated that there will come a degree of activity that we have never had before. It is through the Church Welfare Program that this activity will be stirred. For that reason welfare committees have been organized, composed of eight persons, the Relief Society President being one of them, and representatives of the various Priesthood quorums being the other members.
With the Relief Society president a member of this committee many of the problems now confronting us in connection with relief will be properly solved.
Women have an understanding relative to relief problems that men do not have; consequently it is important that the Relief Society have a definite part in this great program. Me2st of us think of relief in a physical sense. We think that if we administer relief in the form of clothing, fuel and food, that the job is done. As I think of the depression and the effects of it, there are some other forms of relief?forms of relief that have been mentioned by Sister Thompson.
There is a need for spiritual relief, recreational relief, educational relief, cultural relief, and there most certainly is a need for a very RELIEF SOCIETY CONFERENCE close study of the morals of our communities.
These forms of relief will come largely through the Relief Society organization of the Church, and I personally think that they should.
When we think of the finer things in life, such as the spiritual, the educational, the cultural interests, the women of the Church are interested in these perhaps more than the men. I am sure that through the contacts with those who find themselves in destitute circumstances, through the mothers in the homes, opportunity will come to render educational, cultural, and spiritual relief. So, as this great Welfare Program progresses and continues to develop, we are going to find through the Relief Society that there are many problems that we can solve. We know that you are loyal to the Priesthood of the Church; we know you are loyal to the bishops. I never had a more loyal group in all my experience than the Relief Society of my own ward. I remember the time when forty families were thrust upon us through the depression. The first people we called were the sisters of the Relief Society. We formulated a plan and carried it out for General 755 more than a year. It was most successful and the task was well performed because of the cooperation and help we received from this fine organization. We are grateful for what you have done?grateful for this wonderful organization which came to us through the instrumentality of a Prophet of God. It has a purpose and a destiny, and I am sure you are well on your way to the fulfilment of that destiny.
This great organization of mothers promotes encouragement, kindness, liberality, sympathy, tolerance and unselfishness. It has a continual desire to do all that falls within the scope of the good, the true and the beautiful; in fact, I think the thirteenth Article of Faith applies to you and to your objectives: "We believe in being honest, true, chaste, benevolent, virtuous, and in doing good to all men; indeed, we may say that we follow the admonition of Paul. We believe all things, we hope all things, we have endured many things, and hope to be able to endure all things. If there is anything virtuous, lovely, or of good report or praiseworthy, we seek after these things." Session Thursday, a. M., October 6, 1938 TO RAISE HUMAN LIFE TO ITS HIGHEST LEVEL By Counselor Kate M. Barker O the women of the Church has been given a sacred trust, divinely given, which we must maintain and develop that it may be passed on unimpaired in spirit, enlarged in usefulness?"to raise human life to its highest level."
From this obligation nothing can absolve us.
Certainly if we meet our responsibility we must meet immediate present-day needs right around us, in our own community, our own neighborhood. We must get the vision of the Gospel ideal for human life, then we must see conditions as they are.
This we must see and then we must bring to it a new sense of responsibility. Our effectiveness will be in organ- 756 ization. One family cannot do much, but several families can; one group may not be able to do much alone, but all groups interested, if they cooperate and coordinate their efforts, can do much.
When it comes to human welfare who should lead out but the Relief Society whose major interest is human betterment?
Surely if someone takes the initiative, community resources may be so organized that no child need go through life handicapped because of lack of medical care or other necessary advantages.
Another community project might be the control of the type of magazines sold at the news stands in many of our towns. "Such magazines can nearly always be suppressed by the force of public opinion."
Public opinion can only be effective if someone takes the initiative in organizing that opinion to carry over into action. The hunger for friendship is one of the deepest longings of the human soul.
Growth and happiness is dependent upon happy human relations.
Solitary living destroys mental health. We must not interpret our Church Welfare Program altogether in terms of food and shelter; emotional security is equally important.
Neighborliness might well be revived and thus tone up the emotional condition of everyone. OF all relationships in the human world, family relationships are the most everlastingly difficult, and the most permanently rewarding." The greatest growth, the greatest BLESSINGS OF RELIEF RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE happiness can come from those close, intimate relationships of home and family, providing that the family seeks above all else to make life as meaningful as possible for all its members.
Intelligent understanding, sympathy, a high regard for the sacredness of the personality of each member so that each one feels he is wanted, loved and understood, these are the bases for wholesome mental health and happy family life. The family is a fellowship where through sympathy, love, understanding, cooperation, sharing, such strong bonds are built that they can never be broken, developing such companionship that all through life pleasures are enhanced and burdens lightened because they are shared. _ If marriage and family relationships offer such potential joy, should conduct of them be left to chance?
Young people are realizing that the only sensible procedure is to plan and build for happiness on a solid foundation of mutual understanding. To offer help in laying this solid foundation the Relief Society is offering a new course?"Education for Family Life'"?and the first year will be devoted to "Family Relationships." Dr. Paul Popenoe, Director of the Institute of Family Relations of Los Angeles, is the writer of these lessons. May we begin the year's work with the determination that we will so conduct our home life that there will be mutual sympathy and mutual understanding, such peace and joy that the Spirit of our Father in Heaven will dwell therein. SOCIETY MEMBERSHIP By Anna S. Barlow ROBERT LOUIS STEVENSON once said, "When we look into the long avenue of the future and see the good there is for each of us to do, we realize after all, what a beautiful thing it is to work and to live and to be happy."
Every one is striving to live happily']

['Education for Family Life
Family Relationships
Lesson 1
The Family Council Plan
We present as our first topic for discussion in the present series of lessons dealing with family relationships, The Family Council Plan, with the hope that some of our members will become sufficiently interested to adopt it as their pattern for family living. All family life is more or less experimental; therefore, every family who has achieved any degree of success has something of value to contribute to our limited knowledge concerning successful family life. So we suggest that after having given the Family Council Plan a trial over a period of several months an attempt be made to evaluate the experiment on the basis of the primary function of the family, which is the building or molding of personality, and make available to other families our findings that they may profit by our experience.
We understand the Family Council Plan to mean that the family as
496 - JULY, RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE
a group shall participate in the solving of family problems, in determining family policies, in sharing family duties and responsibilities, rights and privileges; that each member of the family be given an opportunity to function in the group according to his ability. We shall refer to this type of living as the democratic form of family life.
A marriage that represents a partnership, in the true sense of the word, between a man and a woman who sincerely love each other, is the foundation stone upon which to build a democratic family organization. In such a family the father and mother serve as the accepted leaders, with each child cooperating with the other members of the group so that all are working together toward the realization of a common objective.
A democracy can succeed to the extent that it is composed of people who are trained and who are willing to occupy positions of leadership and responsibility, and who are conscientiously dependable. The family, as the primary social institution, must provide this necessary training for its members so that the nation can depend upon the family as its constant source of supply for adequate leadership. To rear a child in a democratic family environment offers the greatest possibilities for preparing that child to take his place as an active citizen in a democracy. This accomplishment is the responsibility and duty of every family.
Naturally, because of their maturity, experience, and sympathetic understanding, the parents will assume the position of leadership, in which they will have the responsibility of not only encouraging but also of providing opportunity for the development of initiative, of useful participation, and of voluntary cooperation on the part of each child. If such functioning is experienced, the child will cultivate the essential characteristics for life in a democracy. As some of the most important of these characteristics we list the following: The ability to make intelligent choices, resourcefulness, courage, tolerance, a strong sense of justice, a high regard for the proper use of freedom, and the spirit of sportsmanship.
Because the home has lost many of its former functions, because the family tends to do fewer things as a group that call for sharing of duties and services, and because life in the city offers a comparatively limited number of chores to be done around the home, parents must make the most of every opportunity to make use of the services of the child. This means that when the four-year-old girl says, "Mother, I want to help you with the dusting", the mother will not reply, "Oh, you are such a nice little daughter to offer to help Mother, and I know you are a fine little worker, but you run and play and Mother will do the work." Such a reply offers the easiest course of action, but the wiser way would be for the mother to say, "I do need your help, so I shall show you how to dust all the articles that you are tall enough to reach. I shall do the higher ones, and together we can soon have all the dusting finished." This method will take time and patience on the part of the mother, but the value to the child can scarcely be overestimated. To make use of the child builds up self-confidence. Feeling an active part of the family gives him a sense of security; it gives him an opportunity to experience the satisfaction that comes with the realization that he is a contributing member as well as a receiving member of the group. To deny the child the opportunity to share in family activities gives him a feeling of inferiority because he feels useless. Huxley declares the sense of uselessness is the severest shock which the human system can sustain, and that if persistently sustained it results in atrophy of function. Only as the child takes part in the planning of the family's activities and in the solving of the family's problems will he develop the cooperative behavior pattern necessary for good citizenship in a democracy. The Family Council Plan offers this opportunity; it gives to the parents the privilege to suggest and advise without seeming to unjustly override the wishes of the child.
It is impossible to outline in detail a technique for the procedure of the Family Council that would be workable in all families. The most that can be done is to offer some suggestions that have worked out satisfactorily in some families and which may, with little modification and adjustment, prove helpful to others. Before the family adopts the Council method of control there should be a consensus of opinion that such an arrangement is desirable or at least that all members are desirous of giving it a trial. Without the voluntary cooperation of each one the plan cannot work out successfully.
RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE, JULY - 497
A definite time should be determined when the entire family group can be together for the purpose of discussing affairs of family living. An hour once a week has proved to be the most effective arrangement for most groups. If the meetings are held less frequently it necessitates longer sessions, which is objectionable. The time selected should be when neither the parents nor the children will be too weary or fatigued to take an active part in the discussions. When a regular time has been decided upon, there should be an earnest agreement that each member shall consider the Council meeting the most important engagement for that hour. Some families have found that meal time is the only time when all members of the family can be present; therefore, they discuss their problems at that time. The serious objection to this method is that unpleasant as well as pleasant subjects must be brought up for discussion. Other groups have found Sunday afternoon to be the most feasible time. In considering the plan for adoption it may appear an almost impossible task to find a suitable hour, but in a surprisingly short time it will be looked forward to as one of the most valuable and interesting of all family routine functions and will require little effort to bring the members together.
Preceding the meeting the parents will have anticipated the more important problems to be discussed. At the discretion of the parents they may invite one of the older children to lead the discussion. Many times a problem of such importance will come up that inspiration is
498 - JULY, RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE
needed in its solution. As the problem is discussed and the need for inspiration felt, the family should unite in prayer. An attitude of mutual respect, expressed through courtesy and politeness in the interaction between husband and wife, between parents and children, and between child and child, should be a feature of every family gathering. Every suggestion or idea offered, even that from the youngest member, should receive due consideration.
We shall sum up a few of the benefits to be derived from the Family Council Meetings as follows:
1. Tends to inhibit the parents from making too many choices for the child. Permits the child to do his own choosing as far as possible and thus develops discriminating tastes.
2. Cultivates a more intimate personal relationship between parents and children. Brings to the attention of the child the whys and wherefores of his parents' actions and decisions.
3. Prepares the child to meet his own problems by training him to think through and discuss problems of adjustment.
4. Builds up an appreciation of the value of family unity and solidarity. Thus it gives the child a sense of security and the satisfaction that comes from the feeling that he belongs and is an active part of the family; that he is loved and respected by other members. He responds with the attitude of loyalty and affection.
5. Sets up a common objective so that the group as a whole is conscious of a definite aim towards which they are exerting their energies.
The democratic mode of family life establishes the home as a laboratory for family living, thus better preparing the parents and the children to live more fully each succeeding day by putting greater emphasis on the human relationship values.
Questions and Problems
1. Discuss the activities in which your family as a group has participated during the last month. Who assumed the responsibility for the making and carrying out of the plans?
2. Do you believe that all family problems should be discussed by children and parents? If not, what are some of the problems that should be settled by parents only? Which ones by children only, if any?
3. Should the use of the automobile by John, the eldest of four children be a problem for John and his parents to solve or for the entire family? Who should decide how many party frocks eighteen-year-old Mary should have?
4. A family consisting of father, mother, two daughters, ages 10 and 14 years respectively, and two sons, ages 12 and 16 years respectively, live in a five-room house. They have a small lawn and a small flower garden, and they own an automobile. How would you have each child share in the tasks and responsibilities of the family life?', 'Being of tolerably tough material, I can stand abuse very comfortably. In the place of a Wellesley Trustee, I dare say I should denounce this thievish Harvard professor pretty LESSON DEPARTMENT roundly. And yet I hold that at the present moment I am one of the great benefactors of Wellesley, one of the few who clearly see the direction in which its prosperity lies. "Great causes and great institutions are generally best founded or guided through crises, by a single leader.
They are embodiments of him. His is their inspiration and his their wisdom. The service of them is personal allegiance. To him everything is referred, and his will takes for the time the place of all more minute law and organization.
Wellesley has fortunately had this experience, first under Mr.
Durant and then under the general of his choice. But the danger which besets such an institution is obvious: it does not acquire a life of its own.
Everything is staked on the single leader; and even when that leader is perfect, there is something lacking in the spontaneous vigor of the institution." Miss Freeman continued her work until the Christmas vacation. She was married December 23, 1887, in the home of her beloved friends, Governor and Mrs. Claflin, in Boston. Mr.
Palmer observes that they were married at half past eleven in 593 the morning. Up to that hour of the previous night she worked in her office at the college. Questions 1. To what characteristics do you attribute Alice Freeman's success with young women? 2. To what qualities do you attribute her success as a college president? She might have been successful as a Dean of Women without being a good college president, but she was both. To what qualities do you attribute this success? 3.
State specifically the ways in which she improved Wellesley. 4, What evidence is there that she was deeply religious, and that she depended on her Heavenly Father for help? ' 5.
Discuss the problem of her marriage: In view of the fact that she was performing such a notable service as president of Wellesley, should she have married or should she have remained with the college?
What do you think about it? Note: For additional material consult chapters seven and eight of the "Life of Alice Freeman Palmer" by George Herbert Palmer. SOCIAL SERVICE (For Fourth Week in November) Lesson IT MEETING POVERTY Text: Civic Sociology by Edward A. Ross; Chapters XI and XIII. MONG the problems of social A welfare, poverty is of major importance.
Both an understanding of the various causes which lead to its existence and some scientific knowledge of its relief are necessary for an intelligent approach to the subject.
Effective alleviation necessitates an understanding of its causes and cures. One of society's greatest outlays of energy is the attempt to relieve poverty. But relief without an effort to help those who live in it to gain 594 a position among the socially efficient is futile. Always, there have been many people who have failed to earn the barest necessities of life. As society has advanced in its economic organization the problem of living has become increasingly complex, and the economically unfit have multiplied accordingly. The economic structure now requires greater capacity to meet its problems than ever before, but society has not provided the social machinery to give its relatively less capable members a chance to take their places in its set-up.
With the exclusion of ever-increasing numbers from the ranks of economic production an abnormally large percentage of people have been thrown upon society for their care and upkeep. But this condition is only one of the causes of poverty.
There are many: each leads to a different type; each has a definite place in the social relationships involved as the result of it, and all types must be considered for an understanding of the subject.
This lesson is developed with a consideration of the following points: 1. The acquiring of a more sympathetic understanding of the underlying causes of povetry. 2.
Cultivation of the approval of a standard of living which will lessen the prevalence of poverty. 3. An approach to the methods of alleviating the distress of poverty. An ideal society would be characterized by the absence of poverty and other forms of social maladjustment.
Everyone would be able to make the most of his particular abilities and position. In life. If social institutions were ideally organized and governed, group welfare would consider all individuals; and if all people possessed the ability to meet the demands of the changing social order such a state could exist. HowRELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE ever, present affairs are far from any such Utopianism. Misery, crime, lack of adjustment, and poverty are overwhelmingly present.
Special study is required to appreciate each of these conditions, and poverty presents an intricate problem. Its causes may be thought of under two general headings: First, that which originates among individuals; and second, that for which society is the causative factor. The problem of meeting both devolves upon organized society. To the first group belong those people who live in a state of poverty for biological reasons.
They are the mentally unfit who, nevertheless, are expected to fulfil their obligations in society; those who are chronically unfit physically, and those who are naturally capable but who have been rendered unfit by severe illness themselves or who have had to meet more sickness in their families than they could possibly afford.
Some authorities claim that sickness is the greatest single cause of poverty and that it accounts for 75% of the cases brought to the attention of agencies.
Besides these, there is the great class of the unskilled and of those who are untrained for any vocation or profession.
There are those who have failed to take their places because they are too lazy or too unstable to meet the requirements of their work. And there are those who seem to have a natural propensity for bringing unnecessary disaster upon themselves, such as fires and other losses from sheer carelessness. Then, there are those who are the object of endless grief because they do not know how to handle money.
Improper budgeting of the family income is extremely common. In spite of good intentions many people fail to proportion intelligently their expenditures for food, clothing, LESSON DEPARTMENT shelter, education, pleasure, and additional items. Thus, incomes which should be sufficient are wasted or are used to disadvantage with the deplorable result that children are often.
Deprived of proper preparation for life.
Much needless suffering ensues, and no matter how much such people are helped, poor spending thwarts all efforts towards desirable adjustment unless a thorough reeducation takes place.
Unwholesome appetites lead many to destitution. Liquor, tobacco, excessive and unwise use of leisure are all millstones of poverty.
Improper diet which breaks down health is another cause. And when vanity leads to unwise spending, illicit dealings may follow. To the second group belong the vast numbers of poverty-stricken people who find themselves victimized by the economic and _ social structure; those who are left penniless by the misdemeanors and crimes of those who mishandle trust funds and companies, and those who are thrown out of work by the constant changes in industry and the mechanization of labor.
Some people find it impossible to adjust to the changes in society, and many are excluded from work by the natural rise and fall of the economic curve. In addition to all these there are countless numbers who have been reduced to want through natural causes or unavoidable misfortunes, such as floods, drouth, earthquakes, fires, and other uncontrollable disasters.
Among the first group are those whom we are inclined to call '"God's poor," or the worthy poor—those who have been deprived of their economic independence through no fault of their own— and also the noxious poor. The latter have been made the target for many a sarcastic 595 jibe and story; and truly, they present one of society's most difficult problems. We cannot feel the sympathy for such people that we do for the mentally or the physically unfit.
They are the ones who take advantage of relief at the expense of their self-respect, and those who would take bread from the mouths of others rather than go to work, themselves. However, not all the people who find themselves in need of relief can be classified according to these definite lines and judgments. The matter is far too complicated for any such hard and fast division.
During times of special economic stress such as the world has experienced in recent years, many have been plunged into financial distress and forced to accept temporary relief.
Adjustments to the situation should be accomplished in a short time.
Poor preparation for life is an enormous cause for poverty; its cure can come about only through better education in the home and the school.
Although one of the greatest aims of the educational system is to prepare children for life, thousands are still leaving high schools and colleges unequipped to achieve any degree of independence.
Lack of direction is one of the greatest reasons for this condition. The schools are attempting to overcome it with their plans for individual guidance, but direction in the home is also necessary.
Preparation to take advantage of the opportunities offered in the schools begins long before children reach school age.
Where poor home conditions lead to juvenile delinquency efficient guidance in the school is almost impossible.
Although most secondary schools are handicapped by facilities which are too limited to - insure enough preparation for students to take positions in their chosen 596 vocation immediately after graduation, the opportunities offered do provide a good start towards nearly any kind of training desired. A study of the relief rolls of the past four years reveals the lack of thorough training ina chosen line.
Inadequate and misdirected education will never cease to be a stumbling block in the way of becoming a good producer and provider. The lesson on health pointed out the direct relationship between an unsound body and poverty.
While much sickness is preventable, much could be eliminated through preventive medicine.
With proper training in childhood and youth, much of the poverty that arises for individual reasons might be gradually eliminated, although those cases which are the result of biological reasons are very difficult to solve.
Improvement in environmental and social conditions requires effort which is beyond individual control—the community, state, and nation are here involved.
Despite all the personal ambition in the world, opportunities for accomplishment are sometimes denied.
Inadequate housing, unwholesome environment, faulty government, and abnormal economic cycles are some of the conditions which thwart individual effort and ambition. However, the control of such conditions is to be sought. The solution of such problems forms a large part of the burden of social education, and it depends upon the desire of the people. In considering the second purpose of this lesson, it must be recognized that inasmuch as earning capacities differ, incomes and standards of living must of necessity vary. But, inconsistency between income and standard almost always results in ulRELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE timate poverty. The worship of wealth is not the goal of intelligent or composed living. The higher ideals of religion, spirituality, creative ability, modesty, and intellectuality all suffer because of such false worship. If people could cultivate contentment with what they could afford and relinquish the desire for the material things of life which are beyond their means, there would be less poverty.
Certain commodities, such as an automobile to a physician, are necessities, while in other cases they are luxuries. If a luxury is justified by the income, well and good; if not, it should be eliminated from the expense account. At least peace of mind and more money for bread and butter will follow, even though the comfort and convenience of the luxury is given up.
Effective production in goods and services is the goal which should be sought; and that standard of living which gives the largest margin of production over consumption is the efficient standard for both the individual and the nation. For one person it may mean an expensive level in monetary value, and to another it may indicate very small expenditures.
Family expenses should contribute to the most efficient standard; and efficiency in maintaining one's work and service to mankind is the criterion. An extravagant outlay is a very real setback to many households.
Goods and services of enduring value should not be sacrificed for transient values through lack of planning, haphazard buying, or giving in to momentary enticements, even though one might happen to have the money for such in hand.
Relief of poverty is one of the most difficult problems with which society is confronted. It requires scientific study and administration.
LESSON DEPARTMENT Each step towards the elimination of poverty requires widespread education in regard to its causes and a higher level of human judgment and accomplishment than now exists. But much can be done towards the relief and the prevention of poverty through consistent effort. The odd thing about relief is that much poverty is the direct result of unwise though well intentioned efforts to relieve its suffering. All efforts to help those in need should be preceded by a thoughtful consideration of the phases which follow in the wake of the relief.
There are three points to keep in mind in all relief work: how to relieve, how to rehabilitate the dependent, and how to prevent poverty. Relief, when given, should be carefully supervised, and it should accomplish the purposes for which it is granted.
Promiscuous giving is no longer considered a virtue. The spirit of the Latter-day Saints Church is devoted to the ideal of mutual help in enabling all to meet their problems, rather than the dispensing of charity for charity's sake. Two quotations from the Doctrine and Covenants illustrate this belief: "Thou shalt not be idle; for he that is idle shall not eat the bread nor wear the garments of the laborer." (D. And C. 42:42) "Let every man be diligent in all things. And the idler shall not have place in the Church except he repents and mends his ways." (D. And C. 75:29) There are those who would escape from social problems by resorting to new and untried, or radical forms of government, such as the communists advocate.
Such an approach should be offset by the following statement issued by the First Presidency: "Communism is not a political party - 597 nor a political plan under the constitution; it is a system of government that is the opposite of our constitutional government, and it would be necessary to destroy our constitution before communism could be set up in the United States.
Since communism, established, would destroy our American constitutional government, to support communism is treasonable." The problem of poverty requires the earnest attention of all who are interested in human welfare if the ideal of helping those who are capable of self-respect to achieve it is to be realized. Its relief should not be undertaken by those who do not understand the problem.
Sympathy for the poverty-stricken should be saved for those who deserve it according to the best understanding of this unfortunate state. Activities 1, Evaluate the causes of financial difficulty in your own environment and determine how far an improper standard of living has contributed to the situation.
This activity may be pursued as a community problem or as an individual one. 2.
Make a model budget which would lead to your own financial security. 3.
Through an interview with your bishop and the nearest governmental relief agent, determine the approximate number of people on relief in your vicinity.
What proportion of the cases are due to personal causes, and what proportion are due to the present economic conditions? CHILD GUIDANCE Lesson 2
Can Motuers LEARN More Asout TRAINING THEIR CHILDREN? COMMON _ misconception held by parents is that they do not have to learn how to bring up their children, just as they must learn good manners, the use of the mother tongue, or the preparation of a good meal.
They often imagine that Nature "takes care" of the rearing of children just as it does of begetting them. In the reproductive cycle of insects and some other very simple forms of life, each step in rearing the young seems to be definitely fixed by "instinct".
There is very little evidence that these simple forms of life learn much about the business of being mothers. The case of the human family presents a strikingly different picture. The modern mother's education begins long before the child is born: she learns much about proper diets, calories and vitamins.
After the child is born, she may learn many details of how the baby should be bathed, fed, and clothed. In fact she learns to love this squirming mass of life, her own child. The more she cares for it, the more she loves it.
Before long, the baby becomes more than just a baby: it becomes a person for whom the mother begins to plan for satisfying feelings and attitudes. But much of this fondness is acquired through the intimate contact between the mother and the baby.
Mothers do not have inborn ideas about the details of training their children.
They probably for the most part learn their methods so far as they are at all definite, from their parents, grandparents, and nursemaids.
Inasmuch as ideas about training childern are of human invention, we should not expect them to be more sound than human judgment. Now that modern psychology has shown that adults of middle age are almost as capable of learning new subject matter as their high school children are, parents need have no fear that they cannot learn the necessary facts to become artists at rearing their families.
Parents are slow to improve their methods because they lack the desire to learn.
Mature people have been known to increase their rate of reading a hundred per cent by simply persistenly trying to read faster. The quality of handwriting can likewise be improved, if there is a need for it. In most of our learned activities, we are apt to learn only enough to "get by".
Parents in training their children usually do likewise.
Many mothers are excellent practical psychologists in the sense that they understand the nature of children so intimately that they form the personality of the child as beautifully as the sculptor models his subject. However, by the help of science (which is simply more refined common sense), most mothers could improve their skill in child care and thereby avoid many defects in their children's personalities which might linger as handicaps throughout adulthood. We are taught through modern revelation to seek "diligently and teach one another words of wisdom; yea, seek ye out of the best books words of wisdom; seek learning, even by study and also by faith," and in another connection to "study LESSON DEPARTMENT and learn, and become acquainted with all good books, and with languages, tongues, and people." (D. & C. 88:118; 90:15.) The Lord probably reveals to us only those things that we cannot learn through the ordinary means of study and investigation. The managers of famous race horses, hunting dogs, and prize fighters know that heredity without proper training rarely makes a champion. The arts of training sporting animals are highly developed. But in too many cases the much more important task of training children is extremely thoughtless. Burbank, the great experimenter who has produced so many new varieties of plants, once said, "If we had paid no more attention to our plants than we 'have to our children, we would now be living in a jungle of weeds."
Little do we realize the permanence of some of the scars left in the personality of the child because of our poor methods. In fact, we might go so far as to say that most of the behavior difficulties of children are the outcome of lack of understanding by the parents. If parents would realize how many failures in adult life have their roots in poor management during childhood, how many marriages are unhappy because the partners were not taught as children to live cooperatively with others, or how many adults enter mental hospitals partly because their parents did not understand them when they were children —then the parents would more nearly realize the importance of making enlightened child management the noblest and most painstaking business in life. The proper management of children is an art which is so complex that precise rules cannot always be 599 stated.
Each child must be regarded as unique and as requiring individual treatment. In the fourteen lessons to follow, no pretense will be made at answering all questions about the psychological care of children.
Much will be accomplished, however, if certain general attitudes, principles, and practices are taught concerning some of the most frequently occurring problems. In the first place, we should learn something about heredity and training and how they interact in the development of the child.
Methods of training are frequently seriously warped by wrong ideas as to how these two classes of influences affect the personality of the child. We should know, also, something of the problem of how fears grow in children, what their effect is on the personality.
When they persist, and how they can be overcome. Likewise, anger constitutes an important problem for parents.
What conditions make for anger outbursts? Why do children throw temper tantrums? How can they be overcome? One of the most common practical problems parents have is how to administer rewards and_ punishments.
What are the dangers of whipping? How shall we know how to give praise?
Emphasis will be placed on the importance of good relationships between the parent and child which is necessary for good discipline.
What are some of the causes of persistent bad feeling between parents and their children?
What happens to the personality of the child when he breaks faith with his mother? One of the most difficult adjustments children are required to make is that involving sex. In no field of child training has there been more bungling than in sex instruction. 600
Under this topic, we shall try to learn how children's questions may be answered, and what can be done to direct sex interest into wholesome activity. Attention will be given also to the reasons children tell lies, with particular emphasis on how parents unknowingly make their children lie. With a better understanding of what is back of children's lies, parents should be in a better position to set up the conditions that promote truthfulness for its own sake. Something will be said also about the orderly stages of social development, how children can be prevented from developing mistaken ideas of themselves, what can be done for the child who. Feels inferior, how the child who feels superior should be treated. Because in adolescence we reap the results of mistakes in childhood training, space will be devoted to describing some of the special conflicts of this period of life. When does a person become psychologically mature or truly grown up? What can parents do to make psychological weaning as pleasant as possible for themselves and for the adolescent? How do projected ambitions of parents make weaning difficult? What difficulties sresult from failure to grow up? An effort will be made to give some kind of answer to these and some further questions of more general scope. Better habits in children are posRELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE sible only through improving the habits of the parents. Child guidance begins with correcting the faults of the parents. This is manifestly a most difficult task. However, progress is possible if parents can be given the "will to learn" through being made to sense their responsibility as the real moulders of character. Problems For Discussion 1. What do you consider is the true place of special training in child care? Show how scientific knowledge of child nature is not an adequate substitute for a mother's loving interest in the welfare of the child. 2. State your opinion on the relative importance of some professional knowledge about training children compared with what is known about training animals. 3. In the light of modern revelation, what do you consider is the obligation of Latter-day Saint mothers to study subject matter other than the scriptures? How does this relate to her mission of parenthood? (Doc. And Cov. 88:118.) 4. Compare the amount of learning involved in being a successful human mother with the learning required for the mother robin to successfully rear her young. State a few facts about caring for your children you feel certain you have learned. Mention any elements in your work as mother you think came to you without learning. WE should cultivate a spirit of appreciation and gratitude for others and what they have done for us and mean in our life. Happiness does not consist of what we have but what we are,—Not in our possessions, but in our attitude toward them.—Laura Lim.', 'Problems Of Modern Family Life By Caroline M. Hendricks (Conference Address, Social Welfare Department)
IT is of great significance that such a group of active workers and leaders in this great Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints should meet together for the purpose of discussing family life. The very fact that we are concentrating our attention on a study of family problems should not only prove to be inspirational to each one of us, but it should also enhance our hope, our confidence, and our faith, in a finer type of family life for the future. Undoubtedly our neglect of the study of family life in the past, and our failure to train and prepare our young people for the responsibilities of marriage and parenthood have been largely responsible for the majority of family problems today. That we have met here by appointment to follow a definite course of discussion on family life is evidence that we are cognizant of the great importance of the institution of the family, and also that we are conscious of the fact that family life today is confronted with problems that are a challenge to family stability, problems that are so distressing that some of us are even fearful for the future of family success. If we agree with Charles W. Eliot in his statement that "The security and the elevation of family life are the prime objectives of civilization and the ultimate ends of industry and trade," and again if we are in harmony with Thomas Jefferson who said, "The happiness of the domestic fireside is the first boon of mankind; and it is well that it is so, since it is that which is the lot of the mass of mankind," then we should be justly concerned with this primary institution of society which has always been and must continue to be the foundation stone of our civilization. In considering a discussion of the subject, Problems of Modern Family Life, naturally my first thought was, which of the many problems of modern family life should I select to call to your attention. In answer to this question several other questions presented themselves, such as: Which group in our society is in a position to determine what are the problems of greatest concern; which group will suffer most in the future because of unsolved family difficulties today; which group is invited to give an expression on this subject the least frequently? The answer to these questions undeniably is: The group composed of our sons and daughters who are just approaching the age for marriage; our children who are entering that period in their lives when they will assume the responsibility of founding families of their own; they who will soon be engaged in the process of rearing our grandchildren. So with this answer in mind I decided to get the opinion of such a group and bring it to you this afternoon. Therefore I present to you as first-hand information, what our children think about their homes—the homes which we, as parents, have founded for them.
THROUGH the cooperation of several of my colleagues, I was able to contact some 450 college students. The group, I feel, represent a cross section of the young people of Utah who are attending our institutions of higher learning, inasmuch as they are members of representative families from twenty-seven of the twenty-nine counties in our state. However, in addition there may have been a sprinkling of young folk who came from communities scattered from coast to coast. The group was not selected in any way other than that these students happened to be engaged in class-room work during certain periods last Tuesday morning. The group includes college men and women from freshman to senior rank. These students were asked to list in writing, without signing their names, the three problems in their parental family which they considered to be the most distressing and difficult to handle and were causing the most trouble from a family standpoint. These problems were to be listed in order of their seriousness to family welfare. That only 5 of these 450 college men and women responded to this request in a facetious manner, is, I think, indicative of the fact that our young people have a serious attitude toward family life. Had I the time to give you in detail and in the students' own words some of the many problems as stated by them, you would be greatly impressed and also greatly surprised to learn how keenly the younger generation analyze our efforts of leadership as parents and homemakers and how they view them. But time will permit me to give you only a crude report and
RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE, JUNE - 391
analysis of my findings. I have grouped the various problems into categories, because we wish to reserve a few minutes in which to suggest at least a partial remedy for some of these most vital problems. Approximately 67% of the group listed financial difficulties as the most serious in their family: 250 said the difficulty was a lack of sufficient money, and 42 said there was sufficient income, but trouble was caused by the manner of distribution and lack of wise expenditure of the family income. Many expressed strong resentment against the vicious competitive factors to which we are subjecting our family life. This was indicated in such statements as the following: We cannot afford to have what our friends or our neighbors have; we cannot live up to the standard which mother and father try to maintain; we cannot associate with the people with whom we wish to associate because we cannot afford to do the things they do. Whenever such problems exist in family life I think it indicates a lack of courage on the part of parents to face reality and to determine their own standard of living according to their own incomes and live accordingly, rather than to follow in a herd-minded fashion the standards of living that someone else has set up in their mad effort to keep up with a third family. And so, on and on it goes, the result being a vicious circle of struggle induced by our adherence to false and useless values. Also, it indicates the lack of an appreciation of some of the most valuable elements in living, and those that offer the source of greatest
392 - JUNE, RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE
happiness and satisfaction; namely, our human relationships, our love for one another, the aesthetic values in life.
True it is, though sad, that our young people are so conscious of the lack of money as to consider it the most distressing problem in family life; but this is not surprising when we consider that there are some 11,000,000 young people in the United States today, between 16 and 25 years of age, who are out of school and out of jobs. The seriousness of this condition can only be realized when we pause to consider that these years represent the mating age.
Biologically and chronologically this group of young people are ready for marriage, but economically it seems they have no chance. The second category of problems includes those listed by 53% of the group and expressed in terms of parent-child relationship. The single problem which was mentioned the most frequently was the lack of companionship between parents and children. In many cases it was the mother who was away from home so much of the time that she could not supervise the children. But in the majority of cases it was the father who spent no time with his children. One student said, "We would have no problem if Dad could spend more time at home with us."
Another said, "We need a father more than anything else. When Father is not at work he is in some meeting; we hardly know him."
Still another wrote, "Our problem is the same one most families have—our parents have no time to spend with their children."
This response is in harmony with another study made among 800 young people which showed 61%. I wonder if many children would not be willing and happy to accept the companionship of their parents as a substitute for a part of the lack of money? The third category of problems had to do with the lack of cooperation within the family.
This was listed as the most difficult problem by 36.6% of the group.
This covered a wide field of difficulties.
Among those listed most frequently were: "Mother and father never talk over family problems with us children; we children never know why we are not allowed to do the things we wish to, but are just told no with no explanation given." "Our family never does anything together," was another statement.
Surprising indeed it was to learn that in 10 families the use of the bathroom was their most serious problem. One student wrote, "Our family starts out each day with the whole family quarreling over the use of the bathroom."
This problem may seem trivial to some of us, but it is not trivial when we think of the hazards to family unity and stability involved in a family beginning each day with the entire group quarreling. And more shame to this family and all families who engage in quarreling because of a situation such as the one referred to, one that could be so easily adjusted through cooperation, a little careful management, accommodation of one to another—the mere sacrifice on the part of one or the other, or each in his turn, of arising a few minutes earlier in the morning.
Several students said that in their families one or two members were imposed upon by other members, and this resulted in conflict.
Oftentimes in such cases parents themselves are not aware of the fact that their children feel that they are being treated unjustly because of partiality shown to some member of the group. But because it is what a person thinks is true, whether it be so or not, that influences his behavior, parents should be alert in acquainting themselves with the ideas the child has in regard to the family relationship situation.
This calls for cooperation.
Next came the 20 per cent who listed the husband-wife relationship as causing their most serious difficulty.
This category of problems revealed arguments between parents, lack of consideration of one parent for the other, lack of efficiency, failure of the mother or father to assume his or her share of the family responsibility, and lack of agreement between parents in the handling of their children, such as disciplining them. May I call to your attention just one statement that was recorded in this category: "My father and mother have a regular quarrel every time they decide to go to a movie; they can't agree as to which show to go to, so they always spend about an hour arguing about it, then often it is too late for them to go and each blames the other." In this case the important element is that to the father and mother this repeated occurrence means nothing more than an argument over a movie, but to the children it means a conflict relationship between their father and mother. We must always remember that the seriousness of any family problem must be measured in terms of its effects on the children.
RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE, JUNE - 393
Other problems listed were as follows: Emotional instability of some member of the family was recorded by 60 students; health by 52; religion 34; use of the automobile by 23; death of a parent by 22; liquor and smoking 14; in-laws by 20; and 9 out of the total of 450 reported that they had no serious or distressing problems in their families. I say fortunate indeed are these nine young men and women. I trust that even though we cannot discuss all of these problems, the mere mention of them will result in stimulating many of us to investigate and analyze our own family situation that we may more fully recognize our family problems. This is important because of the fact that the first step in the solution of any problem is to be cognizant of its existence.
HOW I wish that I had a solution to offer for all of these family problems, but that probably will never be possible. The achievement of successful family life will always remain a challenge to even the most wise and intellectual. In the first place, there is no one cause for any family problem; each problem represents a constellation of causes in which there is a sequence of incidents; likewise, there is no panacea or cure-all for any social problem. However, there must be an answer to the economic problem; there must be some way of so distributing our resources that every family will have sufficient income for essentials. When we discover that answer, we will have gone far toward the alleviation of the stress and strain that comes as a result of our imperfect
394 - JUNE, RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE
economic organization.
That our economic structure does not provide economic security for our homes, and that it does not afford our children opportunity for maximum advantages is a fact of which we are all aware. And we agree, no doubt, that one of the most disastrous results of this condition is the fact that such a shortage tends to debase the fundamental ideals of family life. But I wonder if the opinion expressed by our group of students does not offer an indictment against parents from another angle; namely, against the set-up of the present-day standard of values for which we, as parents, are responsible. We cannot and we do not wish to ignore the need for money as a factor in successful living. We do desire to face the reality of the fact that the majority of families in our nation today actually need more than they have in order to provide the essentials, not to mention the cultural advantages.
Even so, is not a great amount of unnecessary stress added because of the fact that we are placing too high a value on the possession of money as such? Do we not need to develop a more Christian attitude toward money? By that I mean, do we not need to look upon money as a commodity, a medium of exchange for the desirable and good things in life, and not as a means of gaining power and prestige?
Should we not aim to develop an appreciation of what money can buy rather than a love of money itself? And at the same time do we not need to emphasize the importance of the type of happiness and success in family life which no amount of money can ever buy? We must place the greater value on the immaterial things in our culture.
Each family must choose its level of life and strive for that level regardless of the level its neighbor is maintaining. And each family must have an appreciation of the desirability of the immaterial things as a source of happiness and satisfaction in family life. One other suggestion I desire to offer in the form of an ardent plea for the democratic form of family organization—family organization based on the marriage of a man and woman who enter into the marriage relation as partners in the true sense of the word, on a 50-50 basis, willing to share equally the sacrifices, the responsibilities, the services, and the love and happiness that such a marriage has to offer—a family in which the council method of management is adopted. The council plan of family management is a subject worthy of the entire afternoon's discussion.
Just briefly I may point out that it calls for regular meetings, at least once a week, at which the entire family group is present.
During these sessions it is the purpose to consider all family problems such as: The family budget, the discipline of members, the use of the automobile, the assignment of duties and privileges to each member according to his age and ability to render service; a standard of values and a goal toward which each member may strive, and which should serve as a guide for the behavior of both the individual and the group. To epitomize, it calls for a frank and honest discussion of all phases of family living with all members of the family taking part in the discussion. I believe sincerely that the family council plan of management offers the most hopeful possibility for the solution of all of the problems which the group of students have called to our attention: Financial, parent-child relationship, lack of cooperation, husband-wife relationship, health, and the others mentioned. The council plan is a significant factor in the democratic type of family.
This form of family organization has been tested and proved to be the most desirable. Of 3,000 marriages of five years or more duration among college educated people which were studied by Dr. Paul Popenoe, with whom you are all familiar, it was found according to the judgment of relatives and close friends, that of the 37 per cent which were of the democratic type of family, 87 per cent were happy; of the 35 per cent wherein the man was dominant, but 61 per cent were happy; and of the 28 per cent wherein the woman was dominant only 47 per cent were happy.
RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE, JUNE - 395
If we are successful in the accomplishment of improving our family life, we must realize at the outset that the future of the family does not rest with fate but lies within our own hands. And that within the family groups of today we have the builders of the family life of tomorrow; that upon the significant developments in the family more than upon the developments of any other institution depends the turning point in all human affairs. In closing may I repeat someone's prayer for the family: Dear God: We thank Thee for our homes. We are glad Thou hast set us in families. Help us to respect all homes. When we have shut the door against all that is without, May we find Thee always within. Speak Thou in our family councils; Let Thy Spirit fix our family choices. Ever be with us in our homes. In sun or shadow be present all our lot to share. Make holy that blest fellowship with those we love. May all homes be havens, since Thou art there. Amen.
God in the Darkness—
Robert E. Speer writes the following: "I was awakened the other morning about four o'clock in my room by a little voice just beside my bed in the dark asking for a drink. I got the little lad a drink, and he lay quiet for a moment, and then asked, 'Father, may I sing myself to sleep?' And I said, 'Yes, dear, go ahead.' But soon he got up so much enthusiasm that I told him he had better stop, or none of the rest of us could sleep. Then he was quiet awhile, but soon I heard his little voice again in the perfect stillness of the night. 'Father, have you got your face turned toward me?' I said, 'Yes, little boy,' and the darkness was as the light of day to him."']

['HAPPENINGS By Annie Wells Cannon THERE would be no past regrets if each day one lived as though it were the beginning of a New Year. COUNTESS WALDECK, Hungarian journalist and traveller discusses in an illuminating lecture "Women Under Dictatorship," how women under Hitler and Mussolini lived under their restricted freedom and comments what significance the position of these women may have in the future of all women. ANTACIO CARMARGO, of New Mexico, 118 years old, has lived under four flags?Spanish, Mexican, Texas-Republic and United States. She is happy to live as long as she can work. NAZIMOVA, Russian ragedienne has returned to the legitimate drama and her favorite role? Ibsen's 'Hedda Gabler." On the opening night in New York she had 38 curtain calls. INA CLAIRE, blonde vivacious actress, during a starring performance of "End of Summer" in Chicago, was told that she had been awarded a gold medal for good diction by the American Academy of Arts and Letters.
After the performance she was officially notified by Dr. William Lyons Phelps. ERNESTINE SCHUMANN HEINK is mourned the world over. Few women have been so renowned and so beolved. Her rich contralto voice thrilled in Grand Opera in the great capitals of Europe and America, cheered soldiers in army camps and sufferers in hospitals. Her motherly heart embraced all mankind in love. KATHERINE CORNELL, in Maxwell Anderson's new drama, "The Wingless Victory" is making her usual success. HATTIE CARAWAY of Arkansas is the only woman in the United States Senate this year, and there are five women in the House of Representatives, while in Brazil where suffrage has maintained only two years there are two women in Congress, ten women Mayors, eight women Consuls and women Secretaries of Labor, Education and Public Health. Mrs. REVA BECK BOSONE is the first woman judge to serve in Salt Lake City. JUDGE FLORENCE ALLEN, of Ohio, in holding Ohio's minimum wage law affecting 42,000 women and minors constitutional, declared the United States supreme court did not criticize the minimum wage law but the method of setting up wage scales. MARGARET FLINT'S _ book, "The Old Ashburn Place," won the $10,000 novel prize this last year and there are other charming novels by women well worth reading. MRS. KATHERINE KENT THURSTON, of Utah, is the recipient of a $60 prize for a poem ?"At Her Wedding."
This poem will be included in an anthology of heretofore unpublished poems. Mrs.
Thurston is a widow and the mother of 10 children. HAZEL HILL GREENWOOD, so deeply mourned by all her associates, gave unsparingly her talents and her service in the work of humanity.  
THE GENERAL BOARD
Amy Whipple Evans Irvine Nettie D. Bradford
RELIEF SOCIETY MAGAZINE
President First Counselor Second Counselor General Secretary and Treasurer Marcia K, Howells Emeline Y, Nebeker Mary Connelly Kimball Janet M. Thompson Belle S, Spafford Donna Durrant Sorensen Mary Connelly Kimball Louise Y. Robison Amy Brown Lyman Vou. XXIV JANUARY, 1937 No.', 'HAPPENINGS By Annie Wells Cannon
MAY—the world brimming over with fragrance, beauty, and song is a testament of God's beneficence.
QUEEN ELIZABETH is her own stylist, and English women take notice. She shunned the extremely short skirts in her wardrobe for the American tour. Did American women take notice?
RUTH BRYAN OWEN RHODE is said to be as eloquent and magnetic as her distinguished father on the lecture platform. Blood will tell.
ELEANOR ROOSEVELT, versatile and accomplished, is a patron of art and never happier than when displaying some of her collections, among them her Goya etchings of which she is especially proud.
LAURA INGALLS, most daring aviatrix since the ill-fated Amelia Earhart, lost somewhere in the South Seas, has planned a solo distance flight record across the Pacific.
MADAME CHIANG KAI-SHEK, touring America in behalf of Chinese refugees, was severely injured in a plane crash. Regaining consciousness she said, "I would gladly die for the cause."
ANNA SCHETININA is the first of the Soviet seagoing women to achieve the rank of Captain, and there are 8,800 women sailors besides over 2,000 women running river boats. Besides the Russians, women in England and other lands are organizing to take over naval jobs in case of war.
MARJORY KINNAN RAWLINGS received the Pulitzer prize of $1,000 for the most distinguished novel of last year, "The Yearling," a pastoral story of a Florida boy.
IDA M. TARBELL has delighted her many admirers by printing "All In The Day's Work," an autobiography. It is the story of a great American who has fearlessly approached life and stood for liberal thought and action. "My Days of Strength" is another interesting biography by Anne Walton Fearn, an American woman doctor who spent forty years in China.
ELIZA A. MUNSON, 91, of Circleville died last month. She was a member of the Daughters of the Mormon Battalion and Daughters of Utah Pioneers and a devoted Latter-day Saint.
THRU L. PARTRIDGE of Utah has recently written a novel "Adventures With A Lamp," the story of a nurse. Elsie C. Carroll is a contributor to "Women Of The West" and Anna Prince Redd's Relief Society contest prize poem "No Beauty Is" appears in "North American Book of Verse," while other anthologies of 1939 are recognizing a number of Utah women poets, among them Nellie Parker, member of the Relief Society General Board, and Mary Leona Jolley, postmistress at Tropic.
